scenarios.title: Situational Awareness
scenarios.summary: Projects that superintelligence is achievable by 2027-2028 through scaling current approaches, triggering an intelligence explosion and intense geopolitical competition.

steep.social: Mass societal disruption as AI surpasses human cognition across all domains; potential for extreme social stratification between those who control AI and those who don't.
steep.technological: Assumes continued exponential scaling of compute and algorithmic efficiency; predicts AGI-level systems within 2-3 years followed by rapid recursive self-improvement.
steep.economic: Economic concentration in whoever achieves superintelligence first; massive value capture by a tiny number of firms; potential for post-scarcity or total economic disruption.
steep.environmental: Massive energy demands for superintelligent systems; environmental impact depends entirely on whether AI is directed toward sustainability or unconstrained growth.
steep.political: Frames as a geopolitical race primarily between US and China; argues for government-lab partnerships and potential nationalization of AI development.

rationale.likelihood: Superintelligence by 2028 is possible but relies on aggressive assumptions about scaling and recursive improvement. Most experts place this as a tail risk rather than base case.
rationale.impact: If realized, impact would be among the most extreme scenarios — near-total economic and social restructuring with minimal time for adaptation.
rationale.preparedness: Essentially no policy infrastructure exists for managing an intelligence explosion. Government awareness is growing but concrete preparations are near zero.

scenarios.title: The Whirlwind: AI's Rapid Transformation
scenarios.institution: Tony Blair Institute for Global Change
scenarios.summary: Argues AI will transform governance, public services and the economy within 5 years, requiring immediate institutional adaptation rather than wait-and-see approaches.

steep.social: Public service transformation creates winners and losers; digital divide deepens unless proactively managed; healthcare and education radically reshaped.
steep.technological: Assumes rapid capability gains in language models enabling automation of most knowledge work; multimodal AI reshaping service delivery.
steep.economic: GDP growth accelerates but labor share of income falls; massive retraining needs; potential fiscal windfall from productivity gains if managed well.
steep.environmental: Relatively optimistic on environmental impact through AI-optimized systems, but acknowledges energy costs of AI infrastructure.
steep.political: Calls for proactive government transformation; new institutions needed; risk of democratic erosion if governments can't keep pace with corporate AI deployment.

rationale.likelihood: Rapid AI-driven government and service transformation is already underway in several countries. The 5-year timeline is plausible given current adoption rates.
rationale.impact: Impact is significant but not catastrophic if managed proactively; main risk is uneven adaptation leaving vulnerable populations behind.
rationale.preparedness: UK and some EU governments have begun adaptation planning, but most lack concrete implementation roadmaps. Digital transformation units exist but aren't AI-focused.

scenarios.title: Machines of Loving Grace
scenarios.summary: Optimistic scenario for powerful AI: if safety challenges are solved, AI could dramatically accelerate progress in health, poverty reduction, governance, and science within 5-10 years.

steep.social: Dramatic improvements in global health and poverty; but benefits depend heavily on equitable access and governance structures.
steep.technological: Assumes powerful AI that is reliably aligned and controllable; human-AI collaboration as primary mode rather than full automation.
steep.economic: Broadly positive economic transformation with AI as amplifier of human capability; acknowledges need for redistribution mechanisms.
steep.environmental: Significant environmental benefits through AI-accelerated clean energy, materials science, and climate modeling.
steep.political: Optimistic about AI supporting better governance, but contingent on solving alignment and maintaining democratic oversight.

rationale.likelihood: Optimistic scenario requires solving alignment, achieving broad capability, AND ensuring equitable deployment — each is uncertain, together they're unlikely in the near term.
rationale.impact: If realized, this would be enormously positive — breakthroughs in health, poverty, and science. Positive impact score reflects the upside.
rationale.preparedness: Some investment in AI safety research and beneficial AI programs, but nowhere near the scale needed to ensure this positive scenario materializes.

scenarios.title: The Compendium: AI & Economic Transformation
scenarios.summary: Comprehensive economic analysis of transformative AI: models GDP growth trajectories, labor displacement timelines, and capital concentration under various automation scenarios.

steep.social: Social disruption concentrated in knowledge-work sectors first; potential for mass purposelessness even with economic safety nets.
steep.technological: Detailed modeling of AI capability trajectories across task types; estimates timeline to full automation of various job categories.
steep.economic: Projects potential 10-30% GDP growth but with severe distributional problems; models show capital owners capturing majority of gains without intervention.
steep.environmental: Notes massive compute energy requirements; models environmental impact of AI infrastructure buildout.
steep.political: Emphasizes need for novel economic policy; existing institutions ill-equipped for speed of transformation.

rationale.likelihood: Epoch's economic modeling is rigorous and their automation timelines track with current capability trends. The distributional crisis is the most likely near-term risk.
rationale.impact: Severe distributional impacts even with GDP growth; the Engels Pause dynamic could destabilize social contracts across advanced economies.
rationale.preparedness: Tax policy, labor market institutions, and social safety nets are designed for a pre-AI economy. Reform discussions are early-stage at best.

scenarios.title: AI 2027: Near-Term Trajectories
scenarios.summary: Detailed scenario modeling the path from current AI to transformative systems by 2027, including lab dynamics, capability jumps, and societal response timelines.

steep.social: Rapid capability gains outpace societal adaptation; potential for crisis moments when AI capabilities visibly surpass human performance on economically important tasks.
steep.technological: Models specific capability milestones: autonomous research by 2026, broadly superhuman reasoning by 2027; assumes continued scaling plus algorithmic breakthroughs.
steep.economic: Economic transformation begins in earnest by 2026 with autonomous AI agents performing professional services; labor market disruption accelerates rapidly.
steep.environmental: Energy infrastructure becomes critical bottleneck; AI companies become largest energy consumers.
steep.political: Government response lags private sector by 12-18 months; regulatory frameworks designed for current AI become obsolete.

rationale.likelihood: Aggressive timeline but from well-informed forecasters. The specific capability milestones are the key uncertainty — if scaling continues, 2027 is not impossible.
rationale.impact: Near-term transformative AI would catch institutions and individuals deeply unprepared, leading to chaotic adjustment period.
rationale.preparedness: Government response timelines of 12-18 months lag mean policies enacted today won't be effective until the transformation is already underway.

scenarios.title: White Collar Wipeout
scenarios.summary: Scenario in which AI rapidly automates professional and knowledge work, creating mass unemployment among previously secure middle-class workers while GDP continues to grow.

steep.social: Middle class social contract breaks down; professional identity crisis; mental health epidemic among displaced knowledge workers.
steep.technological: Assumes AI reaches and exceeds professional-grade performance in law, finance, consulting, medicine, software development within 3-5 years.
steep.economic: GDP grows 5-15% annually but labor share of income collapses; Engels Pause 2.0 where workers become poorer even as economy grows; consumption crisis follows.
steep.environmental: Modest direct environmental impact; potential positive impact from AI-optimized systems offset by growth in compute infrastructure.
steep.political: Fiscal crisis as income tax base erodes; demand for UBI/safety nets surges; political polarization between beneficiaries and losers of AI transition.

rationale.likelihood: Professional job displacement is already visible in coding, legal research, financial analysis. The question is speed and breadth, not whether it happens.
rationale.impact: Middle class destabilization has profound second-order effects: consumption crisis, fiscal crisis, political polarization, mental health epidemic.
rationale.preparedness: Almost no countries have policies specifically designed for rapid white-collar displacement. Retraining programs are scaled for manufacturing job losses, not professional services.

scenarios.title: The Precipice: Existential Risk from AI
scenarios.summary: Frames advanced AI as the most significant existential risk humanity faces this century, with a 10% chance of existential catastrophe from unaligned superintelligence.

steep.social: Existential risk framing implies all social structures at risk; emphasizes intergenerational ethics and responsibility to future generations.
steep.technological: Focuses on alignment problem: sufficiently advanced AI systems may pursue goals misaligned with human values in ways that are catastrophic and irreversible.
steep.economic: Economic considerations secondary to survival; but notes that competitive dynamics create race conditions that increase risk.
steep.environmental: Environmental concerns subsumed under existential risk framing.
steep.political: Argues for unprecedented global coordination; likens to nuclear weapons governance but with harder verification problems.

rationale.likelihood: 10% existential risk this century from AI is Ord's estimate. While this is a low probability, the severity demands attention. Exact probability is deeply uncertain.
rationale.impact: By definition, existential risk represents the maximum possible negative impact — loss of all future human potential.
rationale.preparedness: International AI governance is in its infancy. No equivalent of nuclear non-proliferation frameworks exists for AI. Alignment research is underfunded relative to capability research.

scenarios.title: The Coming Wave
scenarios.summary: Argues AI and synthetic biology represent an unstoppable wave of technology that will fundamentally reshape power structures; calls for 'containment' as the central challenge.

steep.social: Technology diffusion means transformative capabilities become widely accessible, including to malicious actors; societal resilience is the key challenge.
steep.technological: Frames AI as part of a broader wave including synthetic biology; emphasizes the containment problem — keeping powerful tech under control while allowing benefits.
steep.economic: Economic disruption seen as inevitable; focus is on whether institutions can adapt fast enough to manage the transition without catastrophic failures.
steep.environmental: Highlights synthetic biology intersection; environmental risks from both AI energy use and biotech applications.
steep.political: Central argument is about governance: the nation-state may not be the right unit to govern these technologies; calls for new institutional forms.

rationale.likelihood: The 'coming wave' framing is compelling — technology diffusion is historically unstoppable. The containment challenge is real and growing.
rationale.impact: Moderate negative impact reflecting the dual-use nature: significant risks but also genuine potential for benefits if governance catches up.
rationale.preparedness: Some containment thinking is entering policy discourse, but institutional forms for governing dual-use AI remain largely theoretical.

scenarios.title: AI and the Economy: Macro Impacts
scenarios.summary: IMF analysis projecting AI will affect 40% of global jobs, with advanced economies most exposed; warns of significant inequality increases without policy intervention.

steep.social: Advanced economies face highest exposure; developing countries may be shielded initially but miss out on productivity gains; global inequality dynamics shift.
steep.technological: Conservative technological assumptions relative to other scenarios; focuses on current and near-term AI capabilities rather than speculative superintelligence.
steep.economic: Projects 40% job exposure in advanced economies; models fiscal impacts including tax base erosion and social spending increases; emphasizes macro-prudential approach.
steep.environmental: Limited environmental analysis; focuses on economic and fiscal dimensions.
steep.political: Emphasizes multilateral coordination; calls for updating social safety nets, education systems, and tax frameworks across G20 nations.

rationale.likelihood: The IMF's conservative estimate of 40% job exposure is well-supported by multiple analyses. This is arguably the most likely near-term disruption scenario.
rationale.impact: Impact is significant but the IMF framing focuses on manageable transitions — the question is whether policy responds fast enough.
rationale.preparedness: IMF and G20 have begun policy dialogue; some countries are updating labor market and tax frameworks. Most prepared of any scenario, but still inadequate.

scenarios.title: AI and National Security Transformation
scenarios.summary: Analysis of how AI transforms national security landscape: autonomous weapons, cyber capabilities, intelligence analysis, and strategic stability between great powers.

steep.social: Military AI deployment creates new ethical dilemmas; autonomous weapons raise accountability questions; civilian-military AI divide grows.
steep.technological: Focuses on military-relevant AI capabilities: autonomous systems, cyber operations, intelligence processing, decision support for strategic command.
steep.economic: Defense spending restructured around AI; new arms race dynamics; economic impact through military-industrial complex transformation.
steep.environmental: Environmental impact of military AI infrastructure; potential for AI-optimized environmental monitoring but also for AI-enabled resource extraction.
steep.political: Strategic stability between nuclear powers disrupted; AI-enabled first-strike capabilities change deterrence calculus; calls for new arms control frameworks.

rationale.likelihood: Military AI transformation is accelerating across major powers. Strategic stability risks are real but timeline is uncertain.
rationale.impact: National security disruption could cascade into broader instability; autonomous weapons and AI-enabled cyber attacks pose novel threats.
rationale.preparedness: Military AI governance is ahead of economic AI governance in some respects (existing arms control frameworks) but behind in others (no AI-specific treaties).

policies.name: Variable-Rate Token Tax
policies.description: A tax on AI inference compute (tokens) that scales with capability level, creating a new revenue base as traditional income tax erodes. Revenue funds transition programs and public AI infrastructure.

citations.text: Windfall Trust policy brief on token taxation
IMF working paper on digital taxation

policies.name: AI Wealth Tax
policies.description: Progressive tax on AI-derived capital gains and wealth concentration, targeting the extreme value capture by AI company shareholders and compute owners.

citations.text: Piketty on capital taxation in the 21st century
Saez and Zucman on progressive wealth taxation

policies.name: National Skills Transition Fund
policies.description: Government-backed fund providing income support and retraining for workers displaced by AI, with a focus on professional and knowledge workers rather than just manufacturing.

citations.text: OECD skills outlook AI and automation

policies.name: Digital Public Utility Framework
policies.description: Designates critical AI infrastructure as regulated utilities, requiring providers to offer access, maintain standards, and accept oversight in exchange for operating privileges.

citations.text: Windfall Trust Digital Public Utilities paper
Aspen Digital public utility governance models
EU Digital Markets Act as precedent

policies.name: Universal Basic Income Pilots
policies.description: Scaled UBI programs providing unconditional income floors, designed to maintain consumption and social stability during periods of rapid labor market disruption.

policies.name: Public AI Service Corporation
policies.description: A government-backed entity providing AI services for public sector needs — health, education, benefits administration — ensuring critical services aren't dependent on private AI providers.

citations.text: BBC model for public digital infrastructure

policies.name: International AI Safety Research Fund
policies.description: Multilateral fund for AI alignment and safety research, modeled on CERN, ensuring safety research keeps pace with capability development.

policies.name: Democratic AI Oversight Board
policies.description: Independent regulatory body with democratic accountability, technical expertise, and enforcement power to oversee AI deployment in sensitive domains.

policies.name: AI Transition Fiscal Buffer
policies.description: Sovereign wealth fund mechanism that captures AI productivity gains through equity stakes and compute levies, building fiscal resilience before tax base erosion accelerates.

citations.text: IMF fiscal policy in the age of AI

policies.name: AI-Era Job Guarantee
policies.description: Government employer-of-last-resort program focusing on care work, community development, and environmental restoration — sectors where human presence has intrinsic value.

citations.text: Center on Budget and Policy Priorities analysis

policies.name: Compute Commons Initiative
policies.description: Public compute infrastructure ensuring researchers and small organizations can access AI training and inference without dependence on hyperscaler monopolies.

policies.name: Adaptive Education System
policies.description: Reform of education systems to emphasize adaptability and continuous learning rather than fixed credentials, with AI-powered personalized learning pathways.

citations.text: OECD Future of Education and Skills 2030
MIT Work of the Future report

policies.name: AI Arms Control Framework
policies.description: International treaty framework for military AI, establishing verification mechanisms, prohibited applications, and confidence-building measures between major powers.

citations.text: UN Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons AI discussions

policies.name: Citizens Data Dividend
policies.description: Mechanism for citizens to receive payments from AI companies that use public data and publicly-funded research, recognizing collective contribution to AI development.

citations.text: World Economic Forum data governance framework

policies.name: Social Safety Net Modernization
policies.description: Comprehensive reform of benefits systems to handle rapid employment transitions, including portable benefits, income smoothing, and AI-assisted case management.

citations.text: Brookings Institution future of work report
Aspen Institute Future of Work Initiative
